content.text: Business APIs — CRM, calendar, email, messaging, payment — are where LLM agents do real work. An agent that can read a customer record, schedule a meeting, send a Slack message, and look up an invoice is qualitatively more useful than one that only answers questions. Each business API becomes a tool: one function call with typed arguments, returning a structured result the LLM synthesises into its response.
The patterns are consistent across all business APIs:
— every API requires credentials. Store them in environment variables; never hard-code or pass them through the LLM.
— give each tool one responsibility (read a contact, create an event). Avoid god-tools that do five things.
— when modifying data (updating a CRM record, sending an email), pair a read tool with the write tool so the LLM can confirm it has the right record before acting.
— for write tools, design for safe retries. Creating a duplicate meeting is worse than a failed API call.
Write tools that take actions in the real world (send email, create ticket, charge payment) must have an explicit confirmation step unless the user has clearly authorised the action in the same turn.

CRM tools are among the most common LLM business integrations. Typical operations: look up a contact, get company information, create a note, update a deal stage. The HubSpot API is REST-based and uses an API key or OAuth2 bearer token.

Calendar integration lets agents schedule meetings, check availability, and create events. The Google Calendar API uses OAuth2. For server-side use, use a service account; for user-delegated access, use the OAuth2 flow and store the token securely.
Slack tools let agents post messages, look up channels, and search message history. Use a Slack bot token (
) scoped to the minimum permissions needed:

Transactional email tools let agents send confirmation emails, summaries, or alerts. SendGrid is one of the most common providers. Scope this tool carefully: the LLM must never send email to arbitrary addresses or with arbitrary content without explicit user authorisation.
Prevents acting on the wrong record
Check email/channel against an approved set before sending
System prompt: 'Ask the user to confirm before sending any message or creating any record'
Required for destructive or irreversible actions
Return {'error': '...'} instead of raising; tool results must be strings
Broken APIs don't crash the agent loop
Load from env vars; never inject credentials into the prompt
Use read-only tokens where write is not needed
Limits blast radius of a misbehaving agent

Real agent tasks span multiple business systems. An agent given "Send Alice the summary of today's meetings" needs to: list calendar events, format a summary, find Alice's email address, and send it. Wire all the tools together and let the LLM sequence the calls.
Business API tools take real-world actions.

The three safety invariants: credentials never touch the LLM context, write tools always require confirmed intent, and error handling always returns a string (never raises). With these in place, a misbehaving model produces a bad answer — not a data breach or a flood of emails.
